case.title: CASE-01
case.governance: De-identified. No personal identifiers stored anywhere in this workbench. This is decision support — not a diagnosis and not a prescription. The clinical decision belongs to the hemato-oncologist with full context.
case.demographics: Female · elderly (~87y) · sample regions Kropyvnytskyi (Apr) → Kyiv (May). Age is itself a risk modifier for the differential.
case.source: Typed lab reports (ISO 15189), 2 timepoints. Raw CSV: data/CASE-01_labs.csv → data/patient_state.json
case.signal: Point B: serum iron 4.0 LOW + ESR 72 HIGH while ferritin has risen into range (104) — the classic pattern where an acute-phase ferritin masks true iron stores. Flagged for clinician review.
case.discriminating_step: Transferrin saturation (TSAT) + soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) + CRP. sTfR is NOT an acute-phase reactant, so a high sTfR (or sTfR/log-ferritin index) separates true iron deficiency from pure anemia of inflammation — precisely the split this case turns on.

sources.citation: Typed lab reports (ISO 15189), 2 timepoints. Raw CSV: data/CASE-01_labs.csv → data/patient_state.json
Iron Deficiency in the Presence of Anemia and Inflammation — A Sex-Specific Cross-Sectional Study. Eur J Haematol. doi:10.1111/ejh.14431
supports: Ferritin alone is insufficient to assess iron stores when inflammation is present; iron deficiency and anemia of inflammation can coexist and mask each other.

sources.citation: Anemia of Inflammation. Adv Exp Med Biol. doi:10.1007/978-3-031-92033-2_13
supports: Pathophysiology of anemia of inflammation: hepcidin-driven iron sequestration raises ferritin while lowering serum iron and transferrin saturation.

sources.citation: Elderly iron-deficiency anemia → mandatory evaluation for occult GI blood loss (e.g. BSG/AGA guidance).
supports: Microcytic IDA in an 87-year-old requires exclusion of chronic GI blood loss. Reference to be retrieved via the guideline corpus / PubMed connector — not asserted from memory.

sources.citation: Onco-heme vigilance: unexplained persistent inflammation + monocytosis in the elderly → consider MDS / lymphoproliferative / plasma-cell dyscrasia.
supports: A must-not-miss differential in a hemato-oncology setting; specific evidence to be pulled via PubMed / CIViC once smear, LDH, SPEP are available.

hypotheses.label: Iron-deficiency anemia (IDA)
hypotheses.stance: Point A is unambiguous iron deficiency: ferritin 7.5 (clearly low), microcytic/hypochromic indices, high RDW, reactive thrombocytosis (PLT 445).
hypotheses.confirms: Low TSAT + low ferritin + high sTfR
hypotheses.refutes: Ferritin risen to 104 @B — but see P3 (masking).

hypotheses.label: Combined IDA + anemia of inflammation (masked)
hypotheses.stance: The reconciling position: point A is iron deficiency; by point B there is iron repletion of unknown source PLUS a rising inflammatory state (ESR 72). Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant, so its rise to 104 need not mean stores are replete — serum iron is still 4.0. Both processes coexist and mask each other.
hypotheses.confirms: sTfR high (iron-deficient) WITH high CRP (inflamed); TSAT low
hypotheses.refutes: Normal sTfR with high CRP would favour pure inflammation (P2).

hypotheses.label: Anemia of inflammation / chronic disease
hypotheses.stance: Point B shows a marked inflammatory signal (ESR 72, up from 20) with low serum iron and a ferritin that has moved into range — the hepcidin-driven picture of anemia of inflammation.
hypotheses.confirms: High CRP, low TSAT, NORMAL sTfR
hypotheses.refutes: A very high sTfR would betray concurrent true iron deficiency (→ P3).

hypotheses.label: Occult GI blood loss (elderly IDA source)
hypotheses.stance: Microcytic iron-deficiency anemia in an 87-year-old mandates exclusion of chronic GI blood loss regardless of the inflammation question — this is a parallel obligation, not a competing diagnosis.

hypotheses.label: Underlying hematologic malignancy (MDS / lymphoproliferative / plasma-cell)
hypotheses.stance: Onco-heme setting demands vigilance: persistent unexplained inflammation (ESR 72), monocytes at/above ceiling at both points, advanced age. MDS, a lymphoproliferative disorder, or a plasma-cell dyscrasia must be actively excluded.
hypotheses.confirms: Abnormal smear / dysplasia, high LDH, paraprotein on SPEP/immunofixation
hypotheses.refutes: Normal smear, LDH, SPEP and a clear reactive explanation.

labs.note: Δ +55 — major rise; source of recovery unknown (transfusion? iron?)
upper limit at A — reactive thrombocytosis fits iron deficiency / blood loss
monocytes at/above ceiling at both points — worth an absolute count + smear
Δ +52 — marked systemic inflammatory signal at B
rose ~14× — but ferritin is an acute-phase reactant; the rise may be inflammation, not repletion
still LOW at B despite normal-range ferritin — the core dissociation

items.why: iron stores (acute-phase reactant — unreliable when inflamed)
transferrin saturation — key to separate IDA from AI
soluble transferrin receptor — not an acute-phase reactant; the discriminator
C-reactive protein — confirms the inflammatory state
morphology by a hematologist (only a handwritten “aniso/poikilo +++” note exists)
source of chronic blood loss in elderly IDA

methodology.question: Elderly woman, microcytic anemia across two timepoints, with a ferritin/iron/ESR dissociation at point B. What is the differential, and what single test best discriminates it?

questions.q: What drove the Hgb rise 51→106 between A and B?
questions.why: Transfusion vs iron therapy changes how the ferritin at B is interpreted.

questions.q: Are iron deficiency and inflammation coexisting?
questions.why: Ferritin is unreliable when ESR is 72.

questions.q: Source of blood loss / cause of IDA in an 87-year-old?
questions.why: Chronic GI loss must be excluded.

questions.q: Source of the persistent systemic inflammation?
